title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Students will explore the traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of caring for waterways as taonga, connecting the physical health of the water to the hauora of our community. The learning draws on stories of tangata whenua who have protected this awa for generations.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Māori practices for maintaining the mauri (life force) of waterways and connect these to modern health concepts
Conduct water quality testing using both scientific methods and mātauranga Māori observation techniques, recording findings accurately
Collaboratively design and implement a practical action plan to address an identified issue affecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Create a reflective presentation that articulates personal understanding of kaitiakitanga responsibilities and their connection to community wellbeing

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa
activities.description: Students begin at the streamside with a local kaumātua who shares the whakapapa and pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Through storytelling and waiata, students learn about the historical significance of the awa as a source of food, healing, and spiritual connection for Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: Mauri Moana, Mauri Tangata
activities.description: Practical session comparing Western scientific water testing (pH, clarity, nitrates) with Māori observation methods (assessing riparian health, presence of native species, water smell and appearance). Students create dual-data records connecting water health indicators to potential impacts on human health.

activities.title: Hikoi Kaitiaki
activities.description: Guided walk along the awa to identify current pressures (pollution, erosion, invasive species) and existing protection measures. Students map findings using GIS tools and traditional Māori spatial mapping techniques, noting areas of concern and regeneration.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi Restoration
activities.description: Hands-on planting session of native riparian species (harakeke, toetoe) along eroded banks, learning about their natural water filtering properties and cultural uses. Students work alongside local conservation group members practicing proper planting techniques.

activities.title: Whakatō Wairua
activities.description: Reflection circle where students share their learning journey and commit to ongoing kaitiakitanga through creating individual action pledges. These are presented to the group and documented as personal accountability statements.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of traditional water care practices
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water quality monitoring kits and data
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Māori concepts of water rights and water protection'
Native plants from local nursery (Harakeke, Toetoe, Māhoe) for riparian planting

tasks: Water quality testing field report with dual data recording (scientific and cultural observations)
Group presentation of action plan addressing an identified stream issue with clear health connections
Individual reflective journal documenting personal kaitiakitanga journey and learning

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B3 - Interpersonal skills; C3 - Community resources; D3 - Societal attitudes and values
Science: Nature of Science - Investigating in science; Living World - Ecology
Social Sciences: Continuity and Change - Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
